config.writingStyle: A typical episode of Firefly, one of the lighter comedic episodes

data.title: The Cargo of Forgotten Things
npcs: Hoban 'Wash' Washburne (pilot and voice of caution, companion)
Jayne Cobb (muscle with flexible loyalty, companion)
Dr. Simon Tam (ship's doctor and moral compass, companion)
Kaylee Frye (engineer and heart of the ship, companion)
Shepherd Book (spiritual advisor with a dark past, companion)
Mr. Johannsen (nervous client with hidden agenda)

data.title: The Cargo of Forgotten Things
data.premise: You've taken a supposedly simple cargo run to a backwater moon, but the shipment contains more than advertised. When Alliance patrol ships start sniffing around and your cargo begins making strange noises, you realize someone's playing a very dangerous game with Serenity as the board.
data.central_question: Can you deliver the mysterious cargo without getting your crew killed or captured by the Alliance?
data.healing_context: Medical supplies and basic field medicine can treat minor injuries within hours. Serious wounds require Simon's medical expertise and proper rest over days. The infirmary has limited supplies, and severe trauma without proper treatment can be fatal.
starting_inventory: Moses Brothers Self-Defense Engine Frontier Model B pistol
a hip flask with cheap whiskey

setting.name: Persephone Orbital Station and the Rim
setting.description: The story begins at Persephone's bustling orbital station, where legitimate trade mingles with shadier dealings. From there, Serenity must navigate the lawless Rim worlds while avoiding Alliance patrols that seem unusually interested in your cargo.
setting.tone_notes: Despite the comedic banter and crew dynamics, an undercurrent of real danger permeates every decision. The Alliance's presence feels oppressive, and the cargo's true nature creates mounting dread.

acts.title: Simple Job, Complex Problems
acts.description: You accept what seems like a straightforward cargo run, but the client is nervous and the cargo is making unexpected sounds. As you prepare to leave Persephone, Alliance forces begin taking unusual interest in ships leaving the station.
acts.end_condition: Serenity leaves Persephone with the mysterious cargo aboard
locked_constraints: cargo contents cannot be fully revealed
all crew members must remain alive
key_beats: discover cargo is not what it seems
make critical decision about taking the job

acts.title: Hunters and Hunted
acts.description: En route to the delivery point, Alliance ships pursue Serenity while the cargo's true nature becomes increasingly apparent. Crew loyalties are tested as the danger escalates, and you must decide how far you're willing to go to protect whatever's in those crates.
acts.end_condition: the cargo's true contents are revealed to the crew
locked_constraints: cannot reach the delivery destination yet
at least one crew member must be seriously threatened
key_beats: difficult choice about the mission's morality

acts.title: The Price of Doing Right
acts.description: With the cargo's secret exposed and Alliance forces closing in, you must choose between completing the job for the money your crew desperately needs or doing what's right. The final confrontation will test everything you believe about protecting your crew and your principles.
acts.end_condition: final choice made about the cargo's fate
locked_constraints: cannot avoid the central moral choice

npcs.name: Zoe Alleyne
npcs.motivation: protect Mal and the crew at any cost
npcs.secret: suspects the cargo contains people from the beginning but waits for Mal to reach the right conclusion

npcs.name: Hoban 'Wash' Washburne
npcs.motivation: keep everyone alive and get home to his wife
npcs.secret: has been monitoring Alliance communication frequencies and knows they're specifically hunting Serenity

npcs.name: Jayne Cobb
npcs.motivation: get paid and avoid getting shot
npcs.secret: the client offered him extra money to ensure the cargo reaches its destination no matter what

npcs.name: Dr. Simon Tam
npcs.motivation: do no harm and protect his sister
npcs.secret: recognizes medical equipment sounds from the cargo and suspects human experimentation

npcs.name: Kaylee Frye
npcs.role: engineer and heart of the ship
npcs.motivation: keep Serenity flying and everyone happy
npcs.secret: has been hearing voices from the cargo hold but hasn't told anyone yet

npcs.name: Shepherd Book
npcs.role: spiritual advisor with a dark past
npcs.secret: recognizes the client as someone from his past life and knows this job is a trap

npcs.name: Mr. Johannsen
npcs.secret: is trafficking kidnapped children to work in illegal mining operations

npcs.name: Commander Helena Cross
npcs.motivation: capture the traffickers and their cargo
npcs.secret: is actually trying to rescue the kidnapped children and sees Mal as complicit

factions.name: Human Trafficking Ring
factions.description: Criminal organization kidnapping children from outer rim settlements to sell as slave labor
factions.goals: profit from human misery while avoiding Alliance detection

factions.name: Alliance Naval Forces
factions.description: Federal military pursuing the trafficking ring with increasing desperation
factions.goals: rescue the kidnapped children and bring the traffickers to justice

world_rules: The Alliance controls the Core worlds but has limited reach in the Rim
Serenity needs fuel and supplies that cost money the crew doesn't have
Alliance ships are faster and better armed than Serenity
Cargo holds can be scanned but intensive scans take time
The crew's loyalty to Mal is strong but not absolute

- Keep the passage between 150-300 words

- Weave threat-type status effects into the narrative as approaching dangers — build tension as their timers count down
- Focus on prose quality, atmosphere, character voice, and dramatic tension
- Do NOT include state_updates, health numbers, or mechanical data in your response
- Pace the story toward the current act's end condition. Check the CURRENT ACT PACING section for scene count and proximity
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.8, your passage should feel like it's approaching a climax or turning point — increase urgency and stakes
- Introduce key NPCs (antagonists, allies, mentors) naturally throughout each act. Do not wait until the climax to introduce important characters. Check the NPC INTRODUCTION DIRECTIVES section if present.

data.passagePreview: The docking bay on Persephone's orbital station hums with the usual mix of legitimate commerce and l

data.passage:
The docking bay on Persephone's orbital station hums with the usual mix of legitimate commerce and less savory dealings. You adjust your brown coat and check your timepiece—Mr. Johannsen is already ten minutes late for the meet, which in your experience means either the job's about to get more complicated or more profitable. Maybe both.

Zoe stands at your shoulder, her hand resting casually near her sidearm as she scans the crowd. "Don't much like clients who can't keep time, sir," she murmurs, her voice carrying that particular note that means she's already cataloguing exit routes.

Serenity sits powered down in Bay 94, her cargo hold empty and waiting. The crew's scattered about their business—Wash running pre-flight checks, Kaylee sweet-talking the fuel vendor for a better price, and Jayne probably trying to intimidate someone into free drinks. Book's reading in the common area, and Simon's reorganizing his medical supplies for the third time this week.

Your hip flask feels reassuringly solid as you pat it through your coat. Five hundred credits won't keep the ship flying much longer, and this agricultural equipment run to Jiangyin is supposed to be milk money—easy and clean. But when a nervous man in an expensive suit finally hurries toward you, wringing his hands and glancing over his shoulder every few steps, you start to suspect 'easy' just walked out the airlock.

A.text: Greet Johannsen warmly and put him at ease—nervous clients sometimes pay extra for reassurance
A.consequence: Johannsen relaxes slightly, potentially revealing more information about the job
A.narration_directive: Show Mal's diplomatic approach working—Johannsen becomes more talkative but his underlying nervousness about the cargo remains

B.text: Keep your distance and let him come to you—his nervousness might be catching for a reason
B.consequence: Johannsen's paranoia increases, he becomes more secretive about job details
B.narration_directive: Johannsen interprets Mal's caution as suspicion, becomes more guarded and evasive about the cargo specifics

C.text: Signal Zoe to watch the perimeter while you focus on reading Johannsen's body language
C.consequence: Gain tactical awareness of the situation and potential threats
C.narration_directive: Zoe spots suspicious activity—Alliance personnel or other interested parties watching the meeting

D.text: Cut straight to business—time is money and this job's already running behind schedule
D.consequence: Johannsen becomes flustered and makes a critical slip about the cargo's true nature
D.narration_directive: Mal's brusque approach causes Johannsen to nervously reveal something he shouldn't—hint at the cargo containing more than agricultural equipment

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", lethal: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP: The client handles countdown automatically — it subtracts time_elapsed from all timers each turn. Do NOT adjust time_remaining in a modify unless a specific narrative event changes the timeline (e.g. player found a shortcut, magic healing, enemy slowed down). If you adjust a timer, explain why in update_justification.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Physical/mental states — injuries, poisons, buffs, curses, tasks in progress.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers, looming consequences, ticking time-bombs. Examples: pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming storms, spreading fires, political deadlines.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with lethal: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PASSAGE:
The docking bay on Persephone's orbital station hums with the usual mix of legitimate commerce and less savory dealings. You adjust your brown coat and check your timepiece—Mr. Johannsen is already ten minutes late for the meet, which in your experience means either the job's about to get more complicated or more profitable. Maybe both.

Zoe stands at your shoulder, her hand resting casually near her sidearm as she scans the crowd. "Don't much like clients who can't keep time, sir," she murmurs, her voice carrying that particular note that means she's already cataloguing exit routes.

Serenity sits powered down in Bay 94, her cargo hold empty and waiting. The crew's scattered about their business—Wash running pre-flight checks, Kaylee sweet-talking the fuel vendor for a better price, and Jayne probably trying to intimidate someone into free drinks. Book's reading in the common area, and Simon's reorganizing his medical supplies for the third time this week.

Your hip flask feels reassuringly solid as you pat it through your coat. Five hundred credits won't keep the ship flying much longer, and this agricultural equipment run to Jiangyin is supposed to be milk money—easy and clean. But when a nervous man in an expensive suit finally hurries toward you, wringing his hands and glancing over his shoulder every few steps, you start to suspect 'easy' just walked out the airlock.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Greet Johannsen warmly and put him at ease—nervous clients sometimes pay extra for reassurance
B: Keep your distance and let him come to you—his nervousness might be catching for a reason
C: Signal Zoe to watch the perimeter while you focus on reading Johannsen's body language
D: Cut straight to business—time is money and this job's already running behind schedule

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passage:
You flash your most disarming smile and extend a hand toward the twitchy little man. "Mr. Johannsen, welcome aboard. Name's Malcolm Reynolds, Captain of this fine vessel." You gesture broadly at Serenity's cargo bay, where Kaylee's giving the loading crane a fond pat and Jayne's lounging against some crates with his usual scowl.

Johannsen's handshake is clammy, but his shoulders drop a fraction. "Captain Reynolds, yes, thank you for taking this job on such short notice." His eyes dart nervously around the bay before settling back on you. "I must stress the importance of... discretion. The cargo contains, ah, specialized agricultural equipment. Very delicate. Very expensive."

Zoe raises an eyebrow from her position near the loading ramp. Book steps forward with his gentle smile. "Agricultural equipment, you say? Bound for which world, if you don't mind my asking?"

Johannsen's adam's apple bobs. "Whitefall. Remote farming settlement. They're... expanding operations." A metallic thunk echoes from one of his sealed crates, and he jumps like he's been shot. "Just the, uh, hydraulic systems settling. Very sensitive machinery."

Kaylee frowns slightly at the sound, her mechanical intuition clearly pinging something off about that explanation. Simon glances toward the crates with professional curiosity.

"Course, we understand delicate cargo," you say smoothly. "Mind if we take a look at the manifest?"

A.text: Press Johannsen for more details about what's really in those crates
A.consequence: Johannsen becomes defensive and evasive, potentially ending negotiations or revealing he's hiding something dangerous
A.narration_directive: Show Johannsen's panic rising as Mal presses harder—he might bolt or reveal too much about the human cargo

B.text: Accept his explanation and focus on payment terms to avoid spooking him
B.consequence: Negotiations proceed smoothly but crew suspicions remain unaddressed
B.narration_directive: Focus on payment details while crew members exchange meaningful glances about the suspicious cargo sounds

C.text: Let Simon examine the crates professionally—his medical training might spot something
C.consequence: Simon's examination might reveal medical equipment or life signs, forcing a confrontation with Johannsen
C.narration_directive: Simon's medical expertise immediately identifies something wrong with the cargo—possibly life support systems or restraints

D.text: Ask Kaylee her opinion about the 'hydraulic systems' making that sound
D.consequence: Kaylee's mechanical insight provides useful information without directly confronting Johannsen
D.narration_directive: Kaylee diplomatically explains that the sounds aren't from hydraulics, giving the crew more information while keeping Johannsen calm

    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", lethal: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP: The client handles countdown automatically — it subtracts time_elapsed from all timers each turn. Do NOT adjust time_remaining in a modify unless a specific narrative event changes the timeline (e.g. player found a shortcut, magic healing, enemy slowed down). If you adjust a timer, explain why in update_justification.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Physical/mental states — injuries, poisons, buffs, curses, tasks in progress.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers, looming consequences, ticking time-bombs. Examples: pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming storms, spreading fires, political deadlines.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with lethal: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option A.

Narration directive: "Show Mal's diplomatic approach working—Johannsen becomes more talkative but his underlying nervousness about the cargo remains"

- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

You flash your most disarming smile and extend a hand toward the twitchy little man. "Mr. Johannsen, welcome aboard. Name's Malcolm Reynolds, Captain of this fine vessel." You gesture broadly at Serenity's cargo bay, where Kaylee's giving the loading crane a fond pat and Jayne's lounging against some crates with his usual scowl.

Johannsen's handshake is clammy, but his shoulders drop a fraction. "Captain Reynolds, yes, thank you for taking this job on such short notice." His eyes dart nervously around the bay before settling back on you. "I must stress the importance of... discretion. The cargo contains, ah, specialized agricultural equipment. Very delicate. Very expensive."

Zoe raises an eyebrow from her position near the loading ramp. Book steps forward with his gentle smile. "Agricultural equipment, you say? Bound for which world, if you don't mind my asking?"

Johannsen's adam's apple bobs. "Whitefall. Remote farming settlement. They're... expanding operations." A metallic thunk echoes from one of his sealed crates, and he jumps like he's been shot. "Just the, uh, hydraulic systems settling. Very sensitive machinery."

Kaylee frowns slightly at the sound, her mechanical intuition clearly pinging something off about that explanation. Simon glances toward the crates with professional curiosity.

data.passagePreview: You turn to Kaylee, who's been quietly examining the crates with the keen eye of someone who's spent

data.passage:
You turn to Kaylee, who's been quietly examining the crates with the keen eye of someone who's spent her life around machinery. 'Kaylee, what's your take on these hydraulic systems Mr. Johannsen mentioned?' you ask, keeping your tone casual while watching Johannsen's face.

Kaylee brightens at being asked about mechanical matters, but her expression quickly grows puzzled. 'Well, Captain, that's the funny thing,' she says, running her hand along one of the crates. 'These sounds ain't comin' from no hydraulics I ever heard. Hydraulics got a different rhythm to 'em - more of a whoosh and hiss when they're cyclin'. This here...' She pauses as another muffled thump echoes from within. 'This sounds more like somethin' movin' around in there. Somethin' that don't want to be confined.'

Johannsen's face goes pale, and he tugs nervously at his collar. 'Well, perhaps the... the pressure regulators are malfunctioning. These are very sensitive agricultural systems, you understand.'

Simon steps closer, his medical training evident in the way he tilts his head, listening. 'That rhythm,' he says quietly, 'it's almost organic.'

Zoe's hand drifts toward her sidearm, and you can feel the tension in the cargo bay ratchet up another notch. Whatever's in those crates, it sure as hell isn't farming equipment.

A.text: Demand that Johannsen open the crates immediately
A.consequence: Direct confrontation forces Johannsen to reveal partial truth but creates dangerous tension
A.narration_directive: Johannsen reluctantly admits the cargo is 'special' but warns that opening it could be dangerous for everyone

B.text: Ask Simon what he means by 'organic rhythm'
B.consequence: Simon's medical insight provides more clues about the cargo's nature without escalating conflict
B.narration_directive: Simon explains the rhythmic patterns suggest living beings, giving the crew crucial information while maintaining the investigation's subtlety

C.text: Tell Johannsen the deal's off and order him off your ship
C.consequence: Johannsen panics and threatens to expose Serenity to Alliance forces if forced off the ship
C.narration_directive: Johannsen reveals he has Alliance contacts and will report Serenity's location if the deal falls through, creating a dangerous ultimatum

D.text: Quietly signal Zoe to watch Johannsen while you think this through
D.consequence: Strategic positioning gives you tactical advantage while gathering more information
D.narration_directive: Zoe subtly moves into position while you observe Johannsen's increasingly nervous behavior, gaining insight into his desperation

- Healing speed: realistic

Respond with this exact JSON structure:

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", lethal: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP: The client handles countdown automatically — it subtracts time_elapsed from all timers each turn. Do NOT adjust time_remaining in a modify unless a specific narrative event changes the timeline (e.g. player found a shortcut, magic healing, enemy slowed down). If you adjust a timer, explain why in update_justification.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Physical/mental states — injuries, poisons, buffs, curses, tasks in progress.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers, looming consequences, ticking time-bombs. Examples: pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming storms, spreading fires, political deadlines.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with lethal: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You turn to Kaylee, who's been quietly examining the crates with the keen eye of someone who's spent her life around machinery. 'Kaylee, what's your take on these hydraulic systems Mr. Johannsen mentioned?' you ask, keeping your tone casual while watching Johannsen's face.

Kaylee brightens at being asked about mechanical matters, but her expression quickly grows puzzled. 'Well, Captain, that's the funny thing,' she says, running her hand along one of the crates. 'These sounds ain't comin' from no hydraulics I ever heard. Hydraulics got a different rhythm to 'em - more of a whoosh and hiss when they're cyclin'. This here...' She pauses as another muffled thump echoes from within. 'This sounds more like somethin' movin' around in there. Somethin' that don't want to be confined.'

Johannsen's face goes pale, and he tugs nervously at his collar. 'Well, perhaps the... the pressure regulators are malfunctioning. These are very sensitive agricultural systems, you understand.'

Simon steps closer, his medical training evident in the way he tilts his head, listening. 'That rhythm,' he says quietly, 'it's almost organic.'

Zoe's hand drifts toward her sidearm, and you can feel the tension in the cargo bay ratchet up another notch. Whatever's in those crates, it sure as hell isn't farming equipment.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Demand that Johannsen open the crates immediately
B: Ask Simon what he means by 'organic rhythm'
C: Tell Johannsen the deal's off and order him off your ship
D: Quietly signal Zoe to watch Johannsen while you think this through

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

data.passage:
You turn to Simon, whose face has gone pale in the dim cargo bay lighting. "Doc, when you say 'organic rhythm,' what exactly are we talking about here?"

Simon adjusts his sleeves nervously, a habit you've noticed when he's working through medical puzzles. "Well, Captain, hydraulic systems have a very distinct pattern - mechanical, predictable. What I'm hearing is... biological. Heartbeats, breathing patterns, the subtle sounds of living tissue under stress."

Kaylee's eyes widen. "You mean there's something alive in those crates?"

"Someone," Simon corrects quietly. "The rhythm patterns suggest human physiology. Possibly multiple individuals."

Jayne shifts uncomfortably, his hand moving to his gun. "So what, we got stowaways? People trying to hitch a ride?"

Zoe's expression hardens as she catches your eye. "More likely we got ourselves a different kind of cargo run, sir."

Book steps closer to the nearest crate, his weathered hands clasped behind his back. "The question becomes, Captain - are these people passengers or prisoners?"

Wash's voice crackles over the comm from the bridge. "Uh, Mal? You might want to get up here. Alliance patrol just launched from Persephone station, and they're broadcasting some very specific scanning frequencies."

The cargo hold falls silent except for those rhythmic sounds that now seem impossibly loud.

A.text: Head to the bridge immediately to deal with the Alliance patrol
A.consequence: Alliance ships begin active pursuit, forcing immediate evasive action
A.narration_directive: Focus on the bridge tension as Wash reports Alliance ships closing distance and demanding to scan Serenity's cargo

B.text: Confront Johannsen about what's really in the crates
B.consequence: Johannsen panics and triggers a hidden alarm, alerting his associates
B.narration_directive: Johannsen becomes desperate when cornered, revealing he has backup plans and allies monitoring the situation

C.text: Order Simon to examine the crates more thoroughly
C.consequence: Simon discovers the crates contain kidnapped children, providing crucial moral clarity
C.narration_directive: Simon's examination reveals the horrifying truth about human trafficking, galvanizing the crew's resolve

D.text: Tell Jayne to guard Johannsen while you figure this out
D.consequence: Jayne reveals Johannsen tried to bribe him, providing leverage
D.narration_directive: Jayne admits to the bribe attempt, giving Mal insight into Johannsen's desperation and the job's true stakes

DIFFICULTY: Hard

Respond with this exact JSON structure:
{
  "state_updates": {
    "player_changes": {
      "inventory": ["full current inventory list"],
      "skills": ["full current skills list"]
    },
    "status_effect_updates": {
      "add": [
        {
          "id": "string — unique identifier (e.g. broken_arm_001)",
          "name": "string — display name (e.g. Broken Arm)",
          "description": "string — current narrative description of the condition",
          "severity": "number 0.0-1.0 — how debilitating (1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.5 = significant, 0.1 = minor)",
          "time_remaining": "{ days, hours, minutes, seconds } OR null if no auto-expiry",
          "type": "string — 'condition' (default) or 'threat' (approaching dangers that fire when timer expires)",
          "removal_condition": "string describing what removes this effect, OR null if timer-only",
          "on_expiry": "string — REQUIRED when time_remaining is non-null. Narrative directive for what happens when timer reaches zero. Be specific and actionable.",
          "lethal": "boolean — if true, character dies when timer expires unresolved"
        }
      ],
      "modify": [
        {
          "id": "string — id of existing effect to modify",
          "changes": { "severity": 0.5, "description": "updated text" },
          "update_justification": "string — REQUIRED. Why this change is happening (e.g. 'Player rested, wound healing' or 'Ship accelerated, pursuit takes longer')"
        }
      ],
      "remove": [
        {
          "id": "string — id of existing effect to remove",
          "update_justification": "string — REQUIRED. Why removing (e.g. 'Virus deployed successfully' or 'Wound fully healed after rest')"
        }
      ]
    },
    "time_elapsed": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 },
    "event_log_entry": "string — one-line summary of what happened",
    "world_flag_changes": { "flag_name": true/false },
    "relationship_changes": { "npc_or_faction_name": number_delta },
    "npc_updates": { "npc_name": { "role": "string (optional)", "motivation": "string (optional)", "allegiance": "string (optional)", "secret_revealed": "boolean (optional)", "companion": "boolean (optional)", "notes": "string — brief freeform context (optional)" } },
    "new_scene_context": "string — brief context for next passage",
    "location": "string — REQUIRED — current location name (e.g. The Docks, Castle Throne Room, A dark alley)",
    "time_of_day": "string — REQUIRED — one of: dawn, morning, midday, afternoon, evening, night, midnight",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|game_over|hidden_benefit|advances_act|conclusion", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", lethal: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP: The client handles countdown automatically — it subtracts time_elapsed from all timers each turn. Do NOT adjust time_remaining in a modify unless a specific narrative event changes the timeline (e.g. player found a shortcut, magic healing, enemy slowed down). If you adjust a timer, explain why in update_justification.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Physical/mental states — injuries, poisons, buffs, curses, tasks in progress.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers, looming consequences, ticking time-bombs. Examples: pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming storms, spreading fires, political deadlines.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with lethal: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option B.
Pre-classified outcome: ADVANCE_SAFE
Pre-determined consequence: "Simon's medical insight provides more clues about the cargo's nature without escalating conflict"
Narration directive: "Simon explains the rhythmic patterns suggest living beings, giving the crew crucial information while maintaining the investigation's subtlety"

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You turn to Simon, whose face has gone pale in the dim cargo bay lighting. "Doc, when you say 'organic rhythm,' what exactly are we talking about here?"

Simon adjusts his sleeves nervously, a habit you've noticed when he's working through medical puzzles. "Well, Captain, hydraulic systems have a very distinct pattern - mechanical, predictable. What I'm hearing is... biological. Heartbeats, breathing patterns, the subtle sounds of living tissue under stress."

Kaylee's eyes widen. "You mean there's something alive in those crates?"

"Someone," Simon corrects quietly. "The rhythm patterns suggest human physiology. Possibly multiple individuals."

Jayne shifts uncomfortably, his hand moving to his gun. "So what, we got stowaways? People trying to hitch a ride?"

Zoe's expression hardens as she catches your eye. "More likely we got ourselves a different kind of cargo run, sir."

Book steps closer to the nearest crate, his weathered hands clasped behind his back. "The question becomes, Captain - are these people passengers or prisoners?"

Wash's voice crackles over the comm from the bridge. "Uh, Mal? You might want to get up here. Alliance patrol just launched from Persephone station, and they're broadcasting some very specific scanning frequencies."

The cargo hold falls silent except for those rhythmic sounds that now seem impossibly loud.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Head to the bridge immediately to deal with the Alliance patrol
B: Confront Johannsen about what's really in the crates
C: Order Simon to examine the crates more thoroughly
D: Tell Jayne to guard Johannsen while you figure this out

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passagePreview: You point at Jayne, then at Johannsen. "Keep an eye on our client while we sort this mess out." Jayn

data.passage:
You point at Jayne, then at Johannsen. "Keep an eye on our client while we sort this mess out." Jayne nods and positions himself between Johannsen and the exit, hand resting casual-like on his gun. The moment Johannsen sees Jayne approaching, his face goes three shades paler.

"Actually, Cap'n," Jayne says, scratching his head with that particular expression he gets when his conscience is acting up, "there's somethin' I ought to mention. Our friend here pulled me aside before we left dock, offered me an extra five hundred credits to make sure this cargo gets delivered no matter what happens. Said it was 'insurance' against you gettin' cold feet."

Johannsen's mouth opens and closes like a landed fish. "I... that was just... business precaution..."

"Business precaution, my shiny metal ass," you growl. The pieces are falling into place now - the nervousness, the extra payment, the Alliance suddenly taking interest in departing ships. Whatever's in those crates, it's worth enough that Johannsen was willing to buy your muscle's loyalty behind your back.

Wash's voice crackles over the comm: "Mal, those Alliance ships are moving faster now. Whatever you're planning to do, might want to do it quick-like."

A.text: Demand Johannsen tell you exactly what's in those crates
A.consequence: Johannsen reluctantly reveals partial truth about the cargo contents
A.narration_directive: Johannsen breaks under pressure and admits the cargo contains people, but tries to justify it as legitimate transport

B.text: Open the cargo crates yourself to see what you're really carrying
B.consequence: Opening the crates reveals kidnapped children and triggers the end of Act 1
B.narration_directive: Mal opens the crates to discover frightened children inside, forcing an immediate moral reckoning as Alliance ships approach

C.text: Tell Wash to punch it - you'll sort this out in the Black
C.consequence: Fleeing without answers puts the crew in greater danger from both Alliance pursuit and unknown cargo
C.narration_directive: Serenity accelerates into the Black with unresolved tensions and Alliance ships in hot pursuit, making the situation more desperate

D.text: Threaten to space Johannsen unless he comes clean right now
D.consequence: Threatening to space Johannsen triggers Alliance intervention as they detect the confrontation
D.narration_directive: The violent threat is detected by Alliance scanners, escalating their response and putting the crew in immediate danger of boarding
